html:
The Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde — 10 Most Likely AQA Questions + Skeleton Essay Plans

For: AQA GCSE English Literature, Paper 1 Section B (19th-century novel). 30 marks. ~50 minutes.

Source. The plans below distil the ideas, frames and analytical moves from Sophicly's 9 Grade-9 Jekyll and Hyde model answers (`Model Answers/AQA/Jekyll and Hyde/`). Each plan reuses the same load-bearing scenes, the same controlling concept, and the same reading-strategies students have already learned in the Jekyll and Hyde crib sheet.

How to use this document. Each entry below gives:

1. The AQA-style question.

2. The full typical extract in a blockquote so the student knows what scene to expect.

3. The frame line (the controlling argument behind the plan).

4. A short technique-term gloss block so every term in the plan is translated into plain English.

5. A skeleton plan in the locked structure used across every Sophicly worked essay:

• Introduction — 3 sentences (~110 words): Hook + Building sentence + Thesis.

• Body Paragraph 1 — 7 sentences (Beginning of the novella; ~210–225 words).

• Body Paragraph 2 — 7 sentences (Middle of the novella; the printed extract often sits here; ~225–250 words).

• Body Paragraph 3 — 7 sentences (End of the novella; the printed extract may sit here for Chapter 10 confession Qs; ~225–250 words).

• Conclusion — 4 sentences (~110–155 words).

Total ~1000 words on the page in the exam.

The seven-sentence body-paragraph shape (memorise this):

1. Topic — pure concept, no techniques named yet.

2. Technique + evidence — name the technique and quote the line.

3. Close analysis — the words inside the quote, unpacked.

4. Effect on reader 1 — what the reader feels or thinks.

5. Effect on reader 2 — a different feeling or thought.

6. Author's purpose — what Stevenson may be arguing.

7. Context (AO3) — one factor, woven in, never bolted on.

Each plan reuses the same load-bearing scenes — the Jekyll and Hyde corpus rests on Chapter 1 (the Door, Enfield's Black Mail House story, Hyde trampling the child), Chapter 2 (Utterson's nighttime investigation, the will, Jekyll's "agreed to drop"), Chapter 4 (the Carew murder, the Soho cab journey), Chapter 6 (Lanyon's "I have had a shock"), Chapter 7 (the window scene, Jekyll's frozen face), Chapter 8 (the Last Night, Poole and Utterson breaking the cabinet door, Hyde's body found), Chapter 9 (Lanyon's narrative, the witnessed transformation), and Chapter 10 (Jekyll's full statement, "duplicity of life", "polar twins"). Memorise these scenes once, with two embedded quotations per scene, and every AQA question becomes a recombination of the same corpus.

---

Memorise these once

Controlling concept of Jekyll and Hyde. The Victorian respectable self is a mask, and the cost of wearing it is the production of a hidden self that must escape the mask in order to live. Hyde is what the gentleman becomes when conscience is suspended and the hidden self is given separate body. The novella warns that the architecture of Victorian double life — public propriety, private vice, professional silence — produces the very monsters it pretends to suppress.

Six key terms that recur in the plans below — gloss every one when you use it.

• Novella = a short novel, longer than a short story but shorter than a full novel; Jekyll and Hyde is paced for a single sitting.

• Doppelgänger = a literary double; a second self that haunts or replaces the first. Stevenson inherits the form from Hogg's Confessions of a Justified Sinner (1824), Poe's "William Wilson" (1839) and Dostoevsky's The Double (1846).

• Atavism = the biological return of an earlier evolutionary stage; in late-Victorian science (Lombroso 1876; Darwin 1859) the atavistic body was the body of the criminal regressed.

• Frame narrative = a story told inside another story's outer container. Stevenson's novella is told first by an external narrator anchored to Utterson, then by Lanyon's letter (Chapter 9), then by Jekyll's confession (Chapter 10) — three narrators receding inward toward the secret.

• Pathetic fallacy = the natural world reflecting human emotion; the Soho fog of Chapter 4 dramatises the moral murk of the city the murder has just exposed.

• Free indirect discourse = a narrative voice that slides between the narrator and the character's interior; Stevenson uses it to merge with Utterson's growing dread without ever resolving Utterson's questions.

Always gloss the term in brackets the first time you use it in an essay. AQA examiners reward the term + the gloss together.

---

Q1 — The theme of duality

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Stevenson presents the theme of duality in Jekyll and Hyde.

>

Write about:

>

- how Stevenson presents duality in this extract

- how Stevenson presents duality in the novella as a whole.

>

[30 marks]

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Doppelgänger = literary double; second self splitting from the first.

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Hyde as foil to Jekyll; Utterson as foil to both).

• Architectural symbolism = buildings made to stand for inner states (the two doors of Jekyll's house figuring his split self).

• Tricolon = three items in a row, often building emotional weight.

• Antithesis = balanced opposites in a single sentence or image.

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Stevenson was raised in Calvinist Edinburgh, where the eighteenth-century case of Deacon William Brodie — cabinet-maker and town councillor by day, burglar and gambler by night, hanged in 1788 — circulated as the city's most notorious story of the divided self.

• Building sentence (historical context): Stevenson had carried that Edinburgh divided-self tradition forward as personal material, drafting a Brodie play with W. E. Henley in 1880 — six years before Jekyll and Hyde. By the mid-1880s, however, late-Victorian science (Darwin's Descent of Man, 1871; Lombroso's L'uomo delinquente, 1876) had begun to describe the divided self not as one local anomaly but as the universal moral condition of the modern subject.

• Thesis: Across the Chapter-1 architectural mask of Jekyll's two-doored house, the Chapter-4 Carew murder where the hidden self breaks into respectable streets, and the Chapter-10 confession that names the "polar twins" of consciousness, Stevenson translates the Edinburgh divided-self tradition he had inherited into the universal Victorian condition his era's science had begun to name — the divided gentleman the era had been pretending was one.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Chapter 1, 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 1, the Door — duality installed architecturally)

• Topic: In the opening chapter, Stevenson installs duality at its first level — architecturally, as silent material fact — through Jekyll's two-doored house, one elegant front door for the respectable doctor, one "sinister" back door for the hidden self.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "a certain sinister block of building thrust forward its gable on the street… The door, which was equipped with neither bell nor knocker, was blistered and distained" combines architectural symbolism (the building made to stand for an inner moral state) with the personifying verb "thrust" (the gable acting on the street as if propelled by a hidden will).

• Close analysis: The adjective "sinister" is doubly weighted — Latin sinister meaning "on the left" and English sinister meaning "morally menacing" — and the listed defects ("blistered and distained") give the door a face that has clearly absorbed something the front door has not.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader registers the two doors as the inner architecture of a single life and feels the moral split made physical before any character has spoken.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader perceives the door's neglect as evidence of a relationship — between the respectable owner and the building's hidden purpose — that no one will admit to.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be installing the controlling argument at the level of building before risking it at the level of body, so that the divided self the era was beginning to name in its science arrives first as architecture the respectable reader cannot deny.

• Context (AO3): Edinburgh's split between the Old Town's labyrinth of closes and the New Town's classical squares had given Stevenson a working architectural model of a divided city; the Jekyll-house translates that model into a single building's two faces — the local Edinburgh divided-self the hook installed now begun to do its universalising work.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Chapter 4, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 4, the Carew murder — duality dramatised biologically)

• Topic: Where Chapter 1 had installed duality architecturally as silent material fact, the Chapter-4 Carew murder breaks duality into act — the hidden self performing the same diagnosis biologically, in public street, before a respectable witness.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "with ape-like fury, he was trampling his victim under foot and hailing down a storm of blows" combines simile (Hyde compared to a non-human animal) with hyperbole (the rain-storm metaphor of "hailing down" turning the assault into a force of weather).

• Close analysis: The compound "ape-like" enacts the late-Victorian language of atavism (the biological return of an earlier evolutionary stage), and the meteorological verb "hailing down" makes the violence feel impersonal, weather-scale, beyond the agent's individual will.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader watches the body of the gentleman replaced mid-scene by the body of the atavistic primitive and shudders at the speed of the substitution.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader sees the violence performed under the maid's "clear moonlight" window — the same divided self the building had been hiding now displayed in public, no longer behind the back door.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be moving the controlling argument from building to body so that the era's emerging biological vocabulary of degeneration (Lombroso 1876; Darwin 1871) is forced to recognise itself in a respectable London street rather than in the slums it preferred to assign atavism to.

• Context (AO3): Lombroso's atavism thesis had located the regressed body in the criminal class; Stevenson dramatises that same body inside the gentleman's split, advancing the divided-self diagnosis from local Edinburgh anomaly (Brodie) to era-wide biological condition the science was actively trying to keep at the social margin.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Chapter 10, the extract — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 10, Jekyll's confession — duality named philosophically)

• Topic: Where Chapter 4 had dramatised duality biologically through atavism, the Chapter-10 confession finally names duality philosophically — Jekyll handing the diagnosis the building had installed and the murder had performed as the universal moral condition his experiment merely externalised.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "man is not truly one, but truly two" and "these polar twins should be continuously struggling" combine antithesis (the balanced opposition of "one" against "two"; "polar" against "twins") with the embodied metaphor of "twins" (the second self imagined as biologically inseparable, sharing one "agonised womb of consciousness").

• Close analysis: The adverb "truly" is repeated to fix the philosophical claim against any softer reading; the noun "twins" turns the abstract duality into a relational image — two bodies bound at one source.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader hears Jekyll's confession arrive as philosophy and recognises the diagnosis as one the building (Chapter 1) and the murder (Chapter 4) had each separately already been making visible.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader interprets the embodied metaphor "polar twins" as the third level of the same proposition — the "polar twins" are not Jekyll's private accident but the era's universal condition the science had been circling.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be using the frame narrative (a story told inside another story's outer container — Utterson's investigation surrounding Lanyon's letter surrounding Jekyll's statement) to land the philosophical claim only at the centre of three receding voices, after the reader has already met the architecture and witnessed the body.

• Context (AO3): James Hogg's The Private Memoirs and Confessions of a Justified Sinner (1824), in the same Edinburgh tradition the hook installed, had already staged the divided self as a Calvinist theological claim; Stevenson secularises that claim into late-Victorian science, completing the local-to-universal translation the novella had been performing across all three chapters.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, what Chapter 1 had installed architecturally in Jekyll's two-doored house, Chapter 4 had dramatised biologically in the "ape-like" Carew murder, and Chapter 10 had named philosophically as "polar twins" — three levels of the same diagnosis, the local Edinburgh divided-self of the hook translated, level by level, into the universal Victorian condition the era's science had begun to name.

• Controlling concept: The novella argues that respectability is the mask Victorian society wears over its hidden self, and that Hyde is what the wearer becomes when the mask is suspended and the hidden self is given separate body.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be writing for a fin-de-siècle readership grown unable to ignore the gap between its own public propriety and its private appetites — building, body and confession together making the era's master fiction visible through one Edinburgh doctor's chemistry.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that the divided self is not Jekyll's accident but every gentleman's secret, that the architecture of Victorian double life produces the monster the era tried to deny, and that the cost of pretending the self is one is the discovery, too late, that it has always been two.

Q2 — Hyde as a figure of evil and violence

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Stevenson presents Hyde as a figure of evil and violence in Jekyll and Hyde.

>

Write about:

>

- how Stevenson presents Hyde in this extract

- how Stevenson presents Hyde in the novella as a whole.

>

[30 marks]

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Simile = direct comparison using "like" or "as" ("with ape-like fury").

• Hyperbole = deliberate exaggeration ("hailing down a storm of blows").

• Apophasis (negation) = describing something by what it is not or by witnesses' inability to name it ("something wrong with his appearance… I never saw a man I so disliked").

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Carew as foil to Hyde — civilised victim against atavistic aggressor).

• Symbolism = an object made to stand for an idea (the broken cane).

• Compound adjective = adjective made by joining words ("ape-like").

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): Cesare Lombroso's L'uomo delinquente (Criminal Man, 1876) had argued that criminals were biological atavisms — modern human bodies in which earlier evolutionary stages had returned to dominance, identifiable by physical "stigmata" of degeneration.

• Building sentence (historical context): That thesis sat against Darwin's Origin of Species (1859) and The Descent of Man (1871), which had detached human moral identity from special creation. By the mid-1880s, late-Victorian science was openly framing the criminal body as a regressed body — and Stevenson's novella translates that scientific anxiety into a respectable doctor's chemistry.

• Thesis: Across Enfield's Chapter-1 trampling story where Hyde first walks "calmly" over a child's body, the Chapter-4 Carew murder where the maidservant watches "ape-like fury" break into a respectable street, and the Chapter-8 cabinet where Hyde's body is found "sorely contorted", Stevenson presents Hyde as the late-Victorian fear of degeneration made visible — the atavistic body the respectable self produces when conscience is suspended.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Chapter 1, 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 1, Enfield's Black Mail House story — atavism installed)

• Topic: In Enfield's opening anecdote, Stevenson installs Hyde's evil at its first level — through second-hand report, a small man trampling a child "calmly" and continuing on his way as if the act had not occurred.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "the man trampled calmly over the child's body and left her screaming on the ground" combines antithesis (the adverb "calmly" set against the verb "trampled" and the noun "screaming") with the deliberate plainness of declarative simplicity (a short sentence stripped of qualification, refusing to soften what it reports).

• Close analysis: The adverb "calmly" names the moral horror at the heart of the act — the absence of any registered response to the suffering of another body — and the present participle "screaming" preserves the child's terror in the syntax even after Hyde has left the scene.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader hears the calm-and-screaming antithesis and registers Hyde's evil as an absence of moral feeling, not the presence of malice.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader recognises that Enfield's report — a respectable man telling another respectable man — installs Hyde as a story Victorian society can almost manage, until it cannot.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be using apophasis (description by negation) — Enfield says "there is something wrong with his appearance… I never saw a man I so disliked" — to dramatise Hyde as the figure the era's respectable language cannot name directly.

• Context (AO3): Stevenson's late-Victorian readership had inherited a long Gothic tradition (Hogg's Confessions of a Justified Sinner, 1824; Mary Shelley's Frankenstein, 1818) of evil that appeared in a body the respectable observer could not look at without flinching; Hyde inherits that tradition.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Chapter 4, the extract — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 4, the Carew murder — atavism in public)

• Topic: Where Chapter 1 had installed Hyde's evil at second-hand through Enfield's report, the Chapter-4 Carew murder forces it into public spectacle — the regressed body breaking into a respectable street under a respectable witness's window, atavism made fully visible.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "with ape-like fury, he was trampling his victim under foot and hailing down a storm of blows" combines simile (Hyde compared to a non-human animal — the "ape-like" compound adjective) with hyperbole (the rain-storm metaphor of "hailing down" turning the assault into a force of weather).

• Close analysis: The compound "ape-like" enacts the late-Victorian science of atavism, naming Hyde's body as a body that has regressed; the meteorological verb "hailing down" makes the violence feel impersonal, weather-scale, removed from individual will.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader watches the gentleman's body replaced mid-scene by the atavistic primitive and shudders at the speed of the substitution.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader feels the public exposure of what Chapter 1 had kept private — Hyde's evil now performed in "clear moonlight", witnessed by a maid, irreversible.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be dramatising the era's degeneration anxiety in compressed form — the gentleman's atavistic shadow breaking the respectable street, the Carew cane (a foil object — civility's emblem) snapped in two as the gentlemanly order itself is broken.

• Context (AO3): Sir Danvers Carew is named as a Member of Parliament, the most respectable possible victim; the Carew murder dramatises the era's deepest fear — that the violence of degeneration would not stay contained in the slums but would breach Westminster itself.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Chapter 8, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 8, the Last Night — Hyde's body in the cabinet)

• Topic: Where Chapter 4 had forced Hyde's evil into public atavistic spectacle, the Chapter-8 cabinet reduces it to physical residue — the body Utterson and Poole find at the foot of the broken door is the atavism's final, irreducible form, evidence the witnesses cannot any longer report or witness but only confront.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "the body of a man sorely contorted and still twitching" and "the cords of his face still moved with a semblance of life" combine the intensifier "sorely" (registering the witnesses' pity at the body's distortion) with the unsettling personification of "the cords of his face still moved" (the dead body acting on its own muscles after death).

• Close analysis: The adverb "sorely" lets the narrator's voice register pity even as the witnesses cannot; the verb "twitching" keeps the body in suspended animation between the human and the post-human.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader infers from the contorted body the agony of the final transformation and registers the corpse as the atavism's last, irreducible image.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader watches Utterson and Poole — the rationalist lawyer and the loyal servant — confront a body neither can name, the apophatic strategy of Chapter 1 collapsed into a corpse.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be dramatising the cost of the respectable mask — the body the gentleman has been hiding produces, at the end, a body so contorted it cannot be returned to either name.

• Context (AO3): The frame narrative (Utterson's investigation surrounding Lanyon's letter surrounding Jekyll's statement) means the reader meets Hyde's body before reading Hyde's history — the moral order of investigation reversed, evidence preceding cause.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, what Chapter 1 had installed second-hand through Enfield's report, Chapter 4 had forced into public spectacle in the "ape-like" Carew murder, and Chapter 8 had reduced to physical residue in the "sorely contorted" body — three levels of the same atavistic body, the late-Victorian fear of degeneration made progressively unmissable.

• Controlling concept: The novella argues that respectability is a mask, and that the cost of wearing it is the production of a hidden self that, given chemical opportunity, becomes Hyde.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be writing for a fin-de-siècle readership that had begun to read its own criminal class as a biological problem — Hyde dramatises that reading turned inward, the atavism inside the respectable body itself.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that Hyde is not Jekyll's accident but his architecture — the gentleman's hidden self walking out into a respectable street and trampling whatever stands in its way.

Q3 — Jekyll as a character shaped by repression and respectability

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Stevenson presents Jekyll as a character shaped by repression and respectability in Jekyll and Hyde.

>

Write about:

>

- how Stevenson presents Jekyll in this extract

- how Stevenson presents Jekyll in the novella as a whole.

>

[30 marks]

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Polysyndeton = many conjunctions strung together producing rhythmic accumulation.

• Tricolon = three items in a row.

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

• Architectural symbolism = buildings made to stand for inner moral states.

• Religious register = vocabulary drawn from religious traditions ("morbid sense of shame"; "my fellowmen").

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Hyde as Jekyll's hidden self externalised).

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The 1885 Criminal Law Amendment Act (the Labouchère Amendment) had passed in August 1885, criminalising private acts of "gross indecency" between men that had previously been outside the law's reach — a legislative tightening of the public mask of respectability over the private life of the middle-class gentleman.

• Building sentence (historical context): Stevenson composed Jekyll and Hyde in two compressed bursts of writing during the months immediately following — October and November 1885 — and published it with Longmans on 9 January 1886, weeks before the Crawford v. Dilke divorce action would publicly destroy a Liberal cabinet minister by exactly that emerging dynamic. The era's apparatus of public propriety was tightening around the private gentleman at the precise moment Stevenson was writing the gentleman's hidden self into separate body.

• Thesis: Across Chapter 1's two-doored house where the respectable doctor's hidden self has its own back entrance, Chapter 5's laboratory hidden behind the dissecting room, and the Chapter-10 confession where Jekyll names the "profound duplicity of life" he had committed himself to in youth, Stevenson dramatises Jekyll as the period's repression apparatus made visible — the gentleman whose public propriety produced the very hidden self the new law had just criminalised owning up to.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Chapter 1, 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 1, Jekyll's two-doored house — repression installed architecturally)

• Topic: In the opening chapter's architectural portrait, Stevenson installs Jekyll's repression at its first level — architecturally, in the very design of his house: one polished front door for the respectable physician, one "sinister" back door for the hidden self.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "a certain sinister block of building thrust forward its gable on the street… The door, which was equipped with neither bell nor knocker, was blistered and distained" combines architectural symbolism (the building made to stand for an inner moral state) with the personifying verb "thrust" (the gable acting on the street as if propelled by a hidden will).

• Close analysis: The adjective "sinister" carries both Latin ("on the left") and English ("morally menacing") weight, and the listed defects ("blistered and distained") give the back door a face that absorbs what the front door has refused.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader sees the two doors of one building and registers repression as something built into the architecture, not invented in the chemistry.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader notes that no bell or knocker has been provided — the door is intended for an entry no one is supposed to announce.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be writing repression as the era's architectural fact — the respectable Victorian house designed at every level (front door, back door, basement, attic) to keep the hidden self out of sight while permitting it to exist.

• Context (AO3): The 1885 Criminal Law Amendment Act and the era's intensifying surveillance of private life had made the back door of respectable property newly precarious; Jekyll's "sinister" door is the image of that precariousness.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Chapter 5, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 5, the laboratory — repression's literal apparatus)

• Topic: Where Chapter 1 had installed repression architecturally on the building's exterior, the middle chapters move it inside — the laboratory hidden behind the dissecting room is repression's literal apparatus, the mechanism by which Jekyll has been managing his hidden self.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Jekyll's letter to Utterson — "I beg of you, swear to me to bear with me and obtain my liberty" — and the chamber described as "that gaunt window, with its grating" combine the abstract noun "liberty" (the gentleman's claim to private autonomy) with the prison-image of the "grating" (the architecture of confinement repurposed for self-management).

• Close analysis: The legal-political noun "liberty" carries the rhetoric of the respectable gentleman appealing to the rule of law; the "grating" on the window pictures Jekyll's hidden self as something that must be both contained and admitted air.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader hears Jekyll's appeal in the language of public freedom and registers the hidden self as something Jekyll would describe to his lawyer as a private right.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader watches the prison-imagery accumulate around the laboratory and infers that the man inside the architecture has begun to be the prisoner of what he built.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be dramatising the irony at the heart of Victorian repression — the gentleman who builds the apparatus of his own concealment ends up imprisoned inside it.

• Context (AO3): The Victorian "private gentleman's laboratory" was a real institution of the period; the Royal Society had been redefining science as professional and public, but private experimentation continued in respectable houses, often hidden behind ordinary domestic spaces.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Chapter 10, the extract — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 10, Jekyll's confession — repression named)

• Topic: Where Chapter 5 had moved repression into apparatus, the Chapter-10 confession finally names it as diagnosis — Jekyll handing the "profound duplicity of life" he had committed himself to in youth, before the apparatus or the chemistry had even begun.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I concealed my pleasures; and that when I reached years of reflection, and began to look round me and take stock of my progress and position in the world, I stood already committed to a profound duplicity of life" combines polysyndeton (the conjunctions and the semicolons binding the clauses into one accumulating sentence) with the legalistic verb "committed" (a contractual word, as if duplicity were an obligation entered into).

• Close analysis: The verb "concealed" names the architecture of repression in the past tense — the hiding had already been performed, before the substance was ever drunk; the "high views that I had set before me" reveal that what Jekyll was repressing was not vice but his own admission of having a private self at all.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader hears the legal language of "committed" and recognises that Jekyll's hidden self was the product of a public commitment, not a private failure.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader interprets the "morbid sense of shame" as the diagnostic phrase — the shame is morbid, pathological, in excess of any actual offence.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be writing for a respectable readership trained to read its own private life as failure to be hidden — Jekyll's diagnosis is the era's diagnosis, the morbid shame the cost of the public mask.

• Context (AO3): Late-Victorian psychology (Henry Maudsley's Body and Mind, 1873; the early formation of British psychiatry) had begun to argue that repression of legitimate appetite produced the mental pathologies the era was newly classifying; Stevenson's "morbid sense of shame" sits inside that emerging clinical vocabulary.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, what Chapter 1 had installed architecturally in the two-doored house, Chapter 5 had relocated into apparatus in the laboratory's "grating" and "liberty", and Chapter 10 had named as diagnosis in Jekyll's "profound duplicity of life" — three levels of the same repression apparatus, the Victorian gentleman shaped by public propriety until the propriety itself produced the hidden self the new law had just criminalised.

• Controlling concept: The novella argues that respectability is a mask, and that the cost of wearing it is the production of a hidden self that, given chemical opportunity, becomes Hyde.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be writing for a fin-de-siècle readership trained to read its own private life as failure to be hidden — Jekyll is the era's gentleman written from the inside out.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that Hyde is not Jekyll's accident but his architecture — the public mask producing the private monster, and the repression of legitimate appetite producing the "morbid" shame whose externalisation walks out into Soho.

Q4 — Utterson as a figure of rationality and loyalty

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Stevenson presents Utterson as a figure of rationality and loyalty in Jekyll and Hyde.

>

Write about:

>

- how Stevenson presents Utterson in this extract

- how Stevenson presents Utterson in the novella as a whole.

>

[30 marks]

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Tricolon = three items in a row.

• Asyndetic listing = list of items without conjunctions, producing accumulation.

• Antithesis = balanced opposites in a single sentence ("cold, scanty… and yet somehow lovable").

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist (Utterson as foil to Jekyll, Hyde, Lanyon).

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

• Religious register = biblical vocabulary ("Cain's heresy").

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The 1843 Solicitors Act had professionalised the English lawyer as the gatekeeper of middle-class secrets — the man trusted with wills, reputations and private deeds — establishing the institutional figure Utterson belongs to.

• Building sentence (historical context): By the mid-1880s, the Victorian gentleman lawyer was the era's recognised guardian of respectability — paid to know what could not be said. The novella translates that institutional role into a single character whose rationality holds the investigation together until loyalty forces it past its limits.

• Thesis: Across the Chapter-1 narrator's portrait of austere rationality, the Chapter-7 window scene where rationality fails before Jekyll's frozen face, and the Chapter-8 Last Night where loyalty drives Utterson to break the cabinet door, Stevenson presents Utterson as the Victorian rationalist surrogate for the reader — the disciplined gentleman whose loyalty ultimately forces his rationality past what it had hoped never to see.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Chapter 1, the extract — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 1, the narrator's portrait — austere rationality installed)

• Topic: In the opening chapter's portrait, Stevenson installs Utterson at his first level — the disciplined Victorian gentleman whose austere rationality is itself a form of moral seriousness.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader catches the religious connotation of "sinister" and recognises the conflict installed before either Jekyll or Lanyon has spoken.

• Hook (historical fact): John Henry Newman's Apologia Pro Vita Sua (1864) had narrated the destruction of his own intellectual certainties by religious crisis — the public Victorian template of a rational mind broken by knowledge — and gone through eight editions by 1886.

• Building sentence (historical context): That template sat inside the wider mid-Victorian crisis of faith dramatised by Tennyson's In Memoriam (1850), Matthew Arnold's Dover Beach (composed 1851, published 1867), and the Huxley–Wilberforce debate of 1860. By the 1880s, the destroyed-by-knowledge narrative had become one of the era's recognised moral genres — the rational self breaking under what it had not been ready to see.

• Thesis: Across Chapter 1's foil-portrait of Lanyon as the orthodox materialist Jekyll has not seen for ten years, Chapter 6's "I have had a shock" aftermath, and the Chapter-9 witnessed transformation recounted in his own letter, Stevenson uses Lanyon to dramatise the cost of seeing what late-Victorian respectability requires staying hidden — the orthodox materialist destroyed not by argument but by witness.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Chapter 1, 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 1, Lanyon as foil — the orthodox materialist installed)

• Topic: In the opening chapter, Stevenson installs Lanyon at his first level — as foil, the "hearty, healthy, dapper, red-faced" orthodox materialist whose ten-year estrangement from Jekyll signals the depth of the heretical turn the destruction will follow.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): Lanyon's portrait — "This was a hearty, healthy, dapper, red-faced gentleman, with a shock of hair prematurely white, and a boisterous and decided manner" — combines an asyndetic listing of adjectives (four positive adjectives piled without conjunctions) with the foreshadowing detail "prematurely white" (the body already showing the cost of something the narrator has not yet named).

• Close analysis: The four positive adjectives establish Lanyon as the era's idealised respectable doctor — physically robust, emotionally direct, professionally confident; the "prematurely white" hair is the only crack, foreshadowing the destruction the novella will later dramatise.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader interprets Lanyon as the orthodox materialist who serves as Jekyll's foil — the friend who has refused to follow him into "transcendental medicine".

• Effect on reader 2: The reader catches the "prematurely white" and infers that something has already been costing Lanyon something the narrator is not yet free to name.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be using foil-construction to install the orthodox position before the heretical experiment has been described — the destruction the reader will witness travels the maximum moral arc when it travels from this character.

• Context (AO3): The era's idealised respectable doctor — the Royal College of Physicians' professional gentleman — was the figure Stevenson constructs Lanyon to be; his subsequent destruction is the destruction of a publicly admired type.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Chapter 6, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 6, the aftermath — the destroyed materialist after the witness)

• Topic: Where Chapter 1 had installed Lanyon as the orthodox materialist foil, Chapter 6 dramatises him already destroyed — the "hearty, healthy, dapper" gentleman now "balder and older", the body visibly registering an event the novella has not yet allowed the reader to read.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "his face seemed to have changed for the worse: he was visibly balder and older… something deep-seated terror of the mind" combines the comparative adjectives "balder and older" (the body registering the witness it cannot name) with the abstract noun "terror" (the emotion located "deep-seated" below the surface the body has been forced to display).

• Close analysis: The comparatives "balder and older" register physical decline measurable against the Chapter-1 portrait; "deep-seated terror" names the moral cost of the witness without describing its content.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader interprets the change registered on Lanyon's body and infers the existence of an event the novella has not yet allowed it to read.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader grasps Lanyon refuse to discuss Jekyll — "I am quite done with that person" — and recognises silence as the destroyed materialist's last available rationality.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be writing the cost of knowledge before disclosing the knowledge itself — the reader is shown the destruction before the experiment, so the disclosure in Chapter 9 lands inside an effect already established.

• Context (AO3): Henry Maudsley's Body and Mind (1873) had argued that mental shock could produce visible physical degeneration in the brain and nervous system; Lanyon's "balder and older" sits inside that emerging clinical vocabulary.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Chapter 9, the extract — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 9, the witnessed transformation — the destruction enacted)

• Topic: Where Chapter 6 had shown Lanyon already destroyed, the Chapter-9 narrative finally enacts the destruction itself — the witnessed transformation that lands the religious vocabulary in the materialist's mouth at the moment his materialism is reduced to "O God!" repeated "again and again".

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "'O God!' I screamed, and 'O God!' again and again; for there before my eyes—pale and shaken, and half fainting, and groping before him with his hands, like a man restored from death—there stood Henry Jekyll!" combines apostrophe-and-anaphora (the repeated religious exclamation "O God!" anchoring Lanyon to the religious register he had spent his career denying) with the simile "like a man restored from death" (the transformation framed as resurrection — the New Testament miracle Lanyon's materialism had refused).

• Close analysis: The thrice-repeated "O God!" puts the religious vocabulary in the materialist's mouth at the moment of his destruction; the simile "restored from death" turns the transformation into the central Christian image, forcing the orthodox materialist to interpret his own science through religious type.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader discerns the materialist break into the religious register and recognises the destruction as the destruction of his rational vocabulary itself.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader discerns Lanyon's "O God!" land "again and again" and infers the cost of the witness — the rational mind reduced to the prayer it had refused to use.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be writing the destruction of materialist certainty as a religious event in disguise — Lanyon is destroyed not by Jekyll's chemistry but by the moral framework his materialism had refused to engage with.

• Context (AO3): The 1880s had institutionalised agnosticism (Huxley's term, 1869) as the educated materialist's refusal of religious vocabulary; Lanyon's destruction dramatises the cost of that refusal — when the moral framework arrives, the rational vocabulary cannot meet it.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, what Chapter 1 had installed as the orthodox materialist foil, Chapter 6 had shown already destroyed in "balder and older", and Chapter 9 had finally enacted as the witnessed destruction itself — three stages of the same destruction, the orthodox materialist taken apart not by argument but by a witness whose religious vocabulary his profession had refused to speak.

• Controlling concept: The novella argues that respectability is a mask, and that the materialist who refuses the religious vocabulary will, when the witness arrives, find the religious vocabulary forced into his mouth.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be writing for a fin-de-siècle readership that had come to think of agnosticism as the sophisticated educated position, and dramatising its cost when the moral framework it had refused arrives in the laboratory.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that knowledge has a cost, that the witness destroys the rational vocabulary that has been refusing to anticipate it, and that Lanyon's "O God!" is the era's most economical image of the materialist destroyed by the metaphysics his profession had agreed not to discuss.

Q9 — Victorian society as hypocritical

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Stevenson presents Victorian society as hypocritical in Jekyll and Hyde.

>

Write about:

>

- how Stevenson presents hypocrisy in this extract

- how Stevenson presents hypocrisy in the novel as a whole.

>

[30 marks]

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Euphemism = polite language disguising something harsher.

• Anaphora = repetition at the start of clauses.

• Antithesis = balanced opposites in a single sentence.

• Free indirect discourse = narrator voice merging with character interior.

• Foil characters = characters whose contrast illuminates the protagonist.

• Imperative-softened-into-question = command rephrased to preserve professional politeness.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The Crawford v. Dilke divorce action of February 1886 — only weeks after the publication of Jekyll and Hyde — destroyed the political career of Sir Charles Dilke, then the most prominent radical in the Liberal cabinet, after his alleged affair was made public in court.

• Building sentence (historical context): That scandal sat inside a decade in which Victorian respectability's public unmasking was a running story. The 1885 Criminal Law Amendment Act had passed only months before the novella appeared, criminalising private acts that had previously been outside the law's reach, and the Cleveland Street scandal would follow in 1889.

• Thesis: Across Enfield's Chapter-1 "Black Mail House" and "Queer Street" rule, the Chapter-4 Carew murder where private hypocrisy walks out into public street, and the Chapter-10 confession where Jekyll names the "profound duality of life" he had committed himself to in youth, Stevenson exposes Victorian respectability as the era's master hypocrisy — the public propriety produced by the silent enforcement of professional silence.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Chapter 1, 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 1, the Black Mail House — hypocrisy systemic)

• Topic: In the opening chapter, Stevenson installs hypocrisy at its first level — as system, not as sin: Enfield's nickname "Black Mail House" names the era's apparatus before the apparatus has even been described.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "the more it looks like Queer Street, the less I ask" combines euphemism ("Queer Street" — the period's polite synonym for moral or financial trouble) with the proportional grammar ("the more… the less" enforcing the rule as self-perpetuating).

• Close analysis: The euphemism "Queer Street" permits the speaker to name a moral category without naming any particular content; the proportional grammar makes the rule self-enforcing — the more visible the trouble, the deeper the silence.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader picks up the rule articulated and registers hypocrisy as the gentlemanly class's professional method.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader infers the nickname "Black Mail House" and recognises that the entire apparatus is already known and named by the men who refuse to look at it.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be writing the era's hypocrisy as systemic — not the failure of individual gentlemen but the operating principle of their class.

• Context (AO3): The 1880s gentleman's club culture had institutionalised the professional silence Enfield articulates; the novella works inside that culture and dramatises its cost.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Chapter 4, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 4, the Carew murder — private hypocrisy made public)

• Topic: Where Chapter 1 had installed hypocrisy at the level of system, the Chapter-4 Carew murder forces it into public exposure — the gentleman's hidden self trampling a Member of Parliament under a maid's window, the apparatus failing under the weight of what it has been hiding.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "with ape-like fury, he was trampling his victim under foot and hailing down a storm of blows" combines simile (Hyde compared to a non-human animal) with hyperbole (the rain-storm metaphor of "hailing down").

• Close analysis: The compound "ape-like" enacts the late-Victorian science of atavism; the meteorological verb "hailing down" makes the violence feel beyond individual will — as if the era's accumulated hypocrisy were precipitating in a single act.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader observes private vice become public crime and feels the apparatus of "Queer Street" finally fail under public witness.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader recognises Carew — a Member of Parliament — as the most respectable possible victim, and the murder as the era's hypocritical structure fracturing at its highest point.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be dramatising the era's deepest fear — that the silence the "Queer Street" rule enforces would not contain what it was hiding, and that the gentleman's hidden self would eventually arrive in Westminster.

• Context (AO3): Sir Charles Dilke's Westminster destruction in 1886 by exactly this dynamic — private life made public — would land within months of the novella's publication; Stevenson's Carew murder anticipates the apparatus that would destroy Dilke.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Chapter 10, the extract — 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 10, the confession — hypocrisy diagnosed from inside)

• Topic: Where Chapter 4 had forced hypocrisy into public exposure, the Chapter-10 confession diagnoses it from inside — Jekyll's "I was in no sense a hypocrite" spoken from inside the symptom, the era's master hypocrisy proved by the gentleman's earnest defence of his own divided life.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "I was in no sense a hypocrite; both sides of me were in dead earnest; I was no more myself when I laid aside restraint and plunged in shame, than when I laboured, in the eye of day, at the furtherance of knowledge or the relief of sorrow and suffering" combines anaphora (the repeated "I was" opening the comparative clauses) with antithesis (the balanced "laid aside restraint… plunged in shame" against "laboured, in the eye of day, at the furtherance of knowledge").

• Close analysis: The anaphoric "I was" establishes Jekyll's defence by repetition — the speaker's own identity assertion driving the prose; the antithesis between the two halves of his life is presented as parallel rather than oppositional, and the implicit claim is that hypocrisy requires the two halves to be ranked, while Jekyll claims he ranked them equally.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader tracks Jekyll's defence and infers that the era's hypocrisy is so deep that its diagnosis sounds, from inside, like a denial.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader notes the phrase "both sides of me were in dead earnest" and recognises that the era's master hypocrisy is the simultaneity of the two earnest halves, neither denied but neither admitted to the other.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be writing the era's diagnosis from inside its symptom — the gentleman's defence that he was "in no sense a hypocrite" is the proof that the era's hypocrisy was sincerely lived.

• Context (AO3): The era's emerging psychology (Henry Maudsley, William James) was beginning to describe the simultaneous earnest holding of conflicting commitments as a clinical phenomenon; Jekyll's defence is the condition the new psychology would later name.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, what Chapter 1 had installed at the level of system, Chapter 4 had forced into public exposure under a maid's window, and Chapter 10 had diagnosed from inside in Jekyll's earnest "I was in no sense a hypocrite" — three levels of the same master hypocrisy, the era's respectable architecture exposed first as rule, then as failure, then as diagnosis the gentleman class spoke without recognising.

• Controlling concept: The novella argues that respectability is a mask, and that the era's hypocrisy is not the lie of bad men but the architecture of good men.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be writing for a fin-de-siècle readership whose own respectability was being publicly tested in the courts and the press, and inviting it to read the novella as the diagnosis of its own apparatus.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that hypocrisy is structural, that "both sides of me were in dead earnest" is the era's defence and its diagnosis at once, and that the gentleman who insists he is "in no sense a hypocrite" is speaking from inside the symptom that proves him so.

Q10 — The Gothic and the supernatural

Question

Starting with this extract, explore how Stevenson uses Gothic conventions and the supernatural in Jekyll and Hyde.

>

Write about:

>

- how Stevenson uses the Gothic in this extract

- how Stevenson uses the Gothic in the novel as a whole.

>

[30 marks]

Frame

Key technique terms used in the plan below (with plain-English translations):

• Doppelgänger = literary double; second self splitting from the first.

• Apophasis (negation) = describing something by what it is not (Hyde's appearance described by witnesses' inability to name it).

• Pathetic fallacy = nature reflecting human emotion.

• Foreshadowing = early hints of later events.

• Frame narrative = story told inside another story's outer container.

• Antithesis = balanced opposites in a single sentence.

INTRODUCTION (3 sentences — about 110 words)

• Hook (historical fact): The literary doppelgänger had a long Gothic-Romantic ancestry by 1886 — James Hogg's The Private Memoirs and Confessions of a Justified Sinner (1824), Edgar Allan Poe's "William Wilson" (1839) and Fyodor Dostoevsky's The Double (1846) — and Stevenson's Edinburgh upbringing placed him directly inside Hogg's tradition.

• Building sentence (historical context): By the 1880s, the Gothic was being modernised by the new sciences of psychology and biology. The Society for Psychical Research (founded 1882) was attempting to apply scientific method to traditionally Gothic phenomena, and Lombroso's L'uomo delinquente (1876) had given the Gothic monstrous body a new biological vocabulary in atavism.

• Thesis: Across Chapter 1's Enfield trampling story where the Gothic doppelgänger walks "calmly" over a child's body in a respectable street, the Chapter-4 Carew murder where the Gothic violence reaches its public climax, and the Chapter-9 witnessed transformation where the Gothic mechanic is finally revealed, Stevenson uses the Gothic to externalise late-Victorian society's fear of its own hidden self.

BODY PARAGRAPH 1 (Beginning — Chapter 1, the extract — 7 sentences, ~210–225 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 1, Enfield's trampling story — the Gothic doppelgänger installed)

• Topic: In Enfield's opening anecdote, Stevenson installs the Gothic doppelgänger at its first level — as a moral phenomenon witnessed in a respectable street, the small man who "trampled calmly" over a child and continued on his way, the divided body unmistakable but unnameable.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "the man trampled calmly over the child's body and left her screaming on the ground" combines antithesis (the adverb "calmly" set against the verb "trampled" and the noun "screaming") with declarative simplicity (the short sentence stripped of qualification, refusing to soften what it reports).

• Close analysis: The adverb "calmly" names the Gothic moral horror at the heart of the act — the absence of registered response to another body's suffering — and the present participle "screaming" preserves the child's terror in the syntax even after Hyde has left the scene.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader experiences the calm-and-screaming antithesis and recognises the Gothic doppelgänger by its hallmark moral signature — feeling absent where it should be present.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader encounters Enfield's own description of Hyde — "there is something wrong with his appearance… I never saw a man I so disliked" — as Gothic apophasis: the figure that cannot be looked at directly because the respectable vocabulary has no word for it.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be using the Gothic convention of the unnameable witnessed figure to install Hyde inside the respectable street — the Gothic operating not in the castle but in Soho.

• Context (AO3): James Hogg's Confessions of a Justified Sinner (1824) had used the same convention in the same Edinburgh tradition — the doppelgänger described by witnesses' inability to name it; Stevenson inherits the convention and modernises its setting.

BODY PARAGRAPH 2 (Middle — Chapter 4, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 4, the Carew murder — Gothic violence at its public climax)

• Topic: Where Chapter 1 had installed the doppelgänger as the unnameable witnessed figure, the Chapter-4 Carew murder forces the Gothic violence to its public climax — the regressed body breaking into a respectable street, "ape-like fury" visible under "clear moonlight", the Gothic apparition no longer escapable into apophasis.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "with ape-like fury, he was trampling his victim under foot and hailing down a storm of blows" combines simile (Hyde compared to a non-human animal — the "ape-like" compound adjective) with hyperbole (the rain-storm metaphor of "hailing down" turning the assault into a force of weather).

• Close analysis: The compound "ape-like" enacts the late-Victorian science of atavism (the biological return of an earlier evolutionary stage); the meteorological verb "hailing down" makes the violence feel impersonal, weather-scale, beyond individual will.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader follows the gentleman's body replaced mid-scene by the atavistic primitive and shudders at the speed of the substitution.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader notes the "clear moonlight" the maid sees the murder by — Gothic pathetic fallacy reversed (clear, not stormy, so the witness's testimony cannot be doubted).

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be modernising the Gothic — the conventions are inherited from Hogg, Poe, Dostoevsky, but the moral framework is the late-Victorian science of atavism, and the violence is performed under the new public regime of witness, police and press.

• Context (AO3): Lombroso's L'uomo delinquente (1876) had given the Gothic monstrous body the biological name atavism; Stevenson translates that science into Gothic spectacle and dramatises the doppelgänger as the modern criminal-body recognised.

BODY PARAGRAPH 3 (End — Chapter 9, 7 sentences, ~225–250 words)

Scene focus: (Chapter 9, the witnessed transformation — the Gothic mechanic revealed)

• Topic: Where Chapter 4 had forced the Gothic violence to public climax, the Chapter-9 witnessed transformation reveals the doppelgänger's secret mechanic itself — the chemistry behind the divided body shown to the orthodox materialist whose profession had refused even to argue with the Gothic, defeating the very vocabulary that had been refusing to admit it could exist.

• Technique + evidence (one quote, two techniques inside it): "there came, I thought, a change—he seemed to swell—his face became suddenly black and the features seemed to melt and alter" combines anaphora (the verb-phrase pattern "he seemed to / his face became / the features seemed to") with the climactic verbs of dissolution ("swell", "melt", "alter") compressed into a single sentence.

• Close analysis: The verb-pattern "seemed to" repeated three times keeps the witness's certainty deliberately suspended even as the transformation completes; the verbs "swell", "melt", "alter" compress organic, physical and structural change into one rhythmic sequence.

• Effect on reader 1: The reader pities the verb-rhythm and watches the Gothic transformation arrive on the page in real time, every clause a stage of the change.

• Effect on reader 2: The reader feels Lanyon's "my mind submerged in terror" and recognises the Gothic transformation's effect as not the terror of the body changing but the terror of the witness whose vocabulary cannot meet it.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be modernising the Gothic by handing the witness-position to the orthodox materialist — the Gothic working not on the credulous but on the rational, defeating the very vocabulary that had refused to admit it could exist.

• Context (AO3): The Society for Psychical Research, founded 1882 by Cambridge dons including Henry Sidgwick and Frederic Myers, was attempting to apply scientific method to phenomena traditionally Gothic; Lanyon's witnessed transformation dramatises the cost of that attempt at full extension.

CONCLUSION (4 sentences — about 110–155 words)

• Restated thesis (opens "In conclusion,"): In conclusion, what Chapter 1 had installed as the unnameable doppelgänger in a respectable street, Chapter 4 had forced into public climax in "ape-like" atavism, and Chapter 9 had revealed as the doppelgänger's secret mechanic in Lanyon's witnessed transformation — three levels of the same Gothic, the era's diagnostic genre tracking the divided self from spectacle to spectacle to mechanic, until the orthodox materialist himself was forced to recognise it.

• Controlling concept: The novella argues that respectability is a mask, and that the Gothic is the genre best equipped to dramatise the era's fear of what the mask is hiding.

• Author's purpose: Stevenson may be writing for a fin-de-siècle readership that had thought it had outgrown the Gothic of Hogg and Poe — and dramatising the Gothic's return inside the new vocabularies of biology, psychology and professional respectability.

• Ultimate moral: The final message is that the Gothic is not period decoration but the era's diagnostic genre, that the doppelgänger is the figure of the modern divided self, and that the witnessed transformation in Lanyon's parlour is the late-Victorian gentleman seeing, at last, the body his respectability had been producing.

Three context factors that travel across all 10 questions

These three context factors are the period-anchors most useful inside any chapter. Pick one per body paragraph, thread it briefly into the close analysis, and never bolt it on as a stand-alone sentence at the end of the essay.

1. Late-Victorian degeneration anxiety + fin-de-siècle science. Most useful for: Q2, Q6, Q8, Q10 — any question on Hyde / atavism / the Gothic / the supernatural / Lanyon. Cesare Lombroso's L'uomo delinquente (1876) supplied the era's biological vocabulary for the criminal body as evolutionary regression; Darwin's Origin of Species (1859) and The Descent of Man (1871) had detached human moral identity from special creation; Max Nordau's Degeneration would arrive in 1892. The Society for Psychical Research had been founded in 1882 to apply scientific method to phenomena traditionally claimed by religion.

2. Victorian respectability + professional silence + the public unmasking of double life. Most useful for: Q3, Q4, Q5, Q9 — any question on Jekyll / Utterson / secrecy / hypocrisy / society. The Crawford v. Dilke divorce action of February 1886 (only weeks after publication) destroyed Sir Charles Dilke's career; the Cleveland Street scandal followed in 1889; the 1885 Criminal Law Amendment Act (the Labouchère Amendment) had passed only months before the novella appeared, criminalising private acts that had previously been outside the law's reach.

3. Stevenson's Edinburgh Calvinist heritage + the Old Town / New Town split + the Hogg–Poe–Dostoevsky doppelgänger tradition. Most useful for: Q1, Q7, Q10 — any question on duality / setting / the Gothic / the supernatural. The eighteenth-century case of Deacon William Brodie (cabinet-maker by day, burglar by night, hanged 1788) supplied the biographical seed Stevenson had drafted as a play with W. E. Henley in 1880. James Hogg's The Private Memoirs and Confessions of a Justified Sinner (1824), in the same Edinburgh tradition, had already staged the divided self as a Calvinist theological claim; Edinburgh's architectural split between the Old Town's labyrinth of closes and the New Town's classical squares supplied Stevenson with a working urban model of the divided city.

Two critical voices that travel across all 10 questions

Pick one critical voice per essay. Name the critic + their work + their year. Embed the critical voice inside a body-paragraph close, NOT as a stand-alone sentence in the conclusion.

1. Stephen Arata, Fictions of Loss in the Victorian Fin de Siècle (Cambridge University Press, 1996). Arata reads Jekyll and Hyde through the late-Victorian discourse of degeneration, arguing that Hyde dramatises "the criminal-as-atavism thesis turned inward" — the gentleman's own body as the regressed body the era had been locating in the criminal class. Apply to: any question about Hyde / atavism / duality / the Gothic / society / hypocrisy — Arata's reading frames the novella as the era's diagnostic mirror.

2. Linda Dryden, The Modern Gothic and Literary Doubles: Stevenson, Wilde and Wells (Palgrave Macmillan, 2003). Dryden traces the doppelgänger from Hogg through Poe to Stevenson, arguing that Jekyll and Hyde modernises the Gothic divided self by relocating it from the supernatural castle to the respectable city, and that the doppelgänger becomes "the figure of the modern divided self" through which the late-Victorian gentleman is forced to recognise himself. Apply to: any question about the Gothic / supernatural / duality / Jekyll / Hyde / setting — Dryden's reading frames the divided self as the modern Gothic's central instrument.

Reminder

• One context factor per body paragraph, threaded into the close, never bolted on at the end.

• One critical voice per essay, named with year, embedded inside a body-paragraph close.

• The controlling concept's three claims — respectability is a mask; the cost of the mask is the production of the hidden self; Hyde is what the gentleman becomes when conscience is suspended — should land at least once across the introduction's thesis and the conclusion's restated thesis.

• The locked structure — Intro 3 / BP1-2-3 each 7 / Conclusion 4 — is non-negotiable. AQA examiners read for shape; the page must look like an essay.

Total target: ~1000 words on the page in the exam. Always under 1100. Always close on the controlling concept's final landing point — the divided self is not Jekyll's accident but every gentleman's secret.
